    3. Every Brand Should Have A Muse (Introducing Psychographics)
</h2>

  <!-- Attachment Blocks -->
<!-- DEBUG: attachments.count=2 -->

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=17969187, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-17969187">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>Photographers shoot models. That's what they do.</p><p>But SOME photographers are lucky enough to have a muse. This muse is what inspires their best work.</p><p><strong>Every business should have a muse too: their loyal customers.</strong></p><p>Call them your audience, fans, clients, avatar, guests, patients... Whatever you like.</p><p>But for simplicity here, I'll refer to them as your "audience".</p><p>You should understand and love your audience. They give you life.</p><blockquote><strong>“You have to be religious about your audience.” Gary V</strong></blockquote><p>My first year working with The Weeknd was all about the fans. We even started a website for them... about them. We spent 2-3 hours after every show doing meet and greets and autograph signings. </p><p>Serve your audience like that.</p><p>Don't worry about your "followers".</p><p>Your followers are merely people that have "collected you" over time. When it's time for their end-of-year social media clean up, you might be gone like a fad.</p><p>But within all your followers is a devoted audience. They're the people who LOOK for your posts every day. </p><p>They're the ones who would notice if you were gone. </p><p><em>Focus on THEM.</em></p><p>Do you want to go viral?</p><p>Trick question.</p><p><strong>You don't actually want to go viral. That's for attracting the masses. Tons of the wrong people.</strong></p><p>The last thing you want to do is attract everybody. Especially by trying to appeal to <em>everybody</em>. Homogenizing yourself for those who only come to “check you out” doesn't help you in the long run. Those are the passer-bys... the window shoppers. <a href="https://kk.org/thetechnium/1000-true-fans/" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Play for your "true fans" and they'll tell your FUTURE "true fans" about you.</a></p><p>And yes, if you do this well, you'll get haters.</p><p>Trust me... </p><p><em>Haters are your second-best spokespeople. </em></p><p>My dad always says, “For every person who hates what you do... there's someone who loves it BECAUSE the other person hates it.” </p><p>The noise the haters make to criticize you will be the best PR to attract even more of “your people”.</p><p>If you want to grow faster than everyone else you have to ignore all the stuff that everyone else is obsessed about. All of that just feeds your ego and you just do it for outside perception. Obsess about your audience instead. Do more of what's foundational. Add value to your customer's lives and you win. Even if you're not on Snapchat or Fakebook or a part of the Instagram mafia. </p><p>But let's dig deeper here. </p><p>Do you even know who "your people" are?</p><p>So many businesses think it's their "customer demographic".</p><p>Guys, we're not in the MySpace era. </p><p>Today, in order for you to use all these digital marketing tools more effectively... you're going to have to wrap your head around something more powerful: <em>psychographics. </em></p><p><br></p><h2>Psychographics</h2><p>Ok. Before we get all poetic about it,  you should answer basic demographic questions. You should know things about your audience like: gender, where they're from, their race, religion etc... </p><p>Don't be afraid to EXCLUDE. You want to EXCLUDE. Who's your audience? Men or women? Yeah, yeah, I know it's both. But who is it REALLY (it doesn't mean others won't like it -- I'm asking you who are you making it for?) Narrow it down. Are you making this for older people or younger people? Urban people or country people.</p><p>Next, you figure out the psychographics of your audience. In todays world psychographics are MORE important than demographics.</p><p>Demographics is data on things you can see.</p><p><strong>Psychographics is about knowing how your audience thinks and feels.</strong></p><p>To figure out your audience's psychographics, you need to engage in a little method acting. Imagine "your people" and then you imagine what they want. What websites do they go to online? What food do they eat? Are they spiritual? Do they like video games? What kind of causes would they march for? You should know what they dream of and what would make their day. </p><p>And then from now on, when you communicate with them... Do it on their level. Talk to them in the way that you KNOW that they'd be most receptive... every single time.</p><p>You need to know your audience better than they know themselves.  </p><p>You need to be like Bill Clinton back in '92 fighting to win the presidency.</p><p>"I feel your painnnnnn..."</p><p>Find 10 people to create for. Perfect your craft until they ALL wanna scream and tell everybody they know about you! </p><p><em>Those people will lead you to the next 100... and then the next 1000… </em></p><p><strong>I mean...If you can't attract ten die-hards then how are you planning to rule the world?</strong></p><p>When communication is tailor-made for "your people" you pretty much never have to hype anything or use manipulation “tactics“ like the others do. </p><p>Hearing from you... becomes (gasp) desirable!</p><p>You become your own "word of mouth". They trust you THAT much.</p><p>If you don’t believe me read the Bezos letters.</p><p>Did you know that Jeff Bezos (the owner of Amazon) wrote an <a href="https://drive.google.com/file/d/1SpgDsIpC_cAS0O4cBz4Sb_GJcEIBhUtA/view" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">end of year email to all his employees detailing his complete vision for the future... every year since the beginning</a>?</p><p>Do you realize that everything he wrote came true? </p><p>Guess what the core principle of all those emails were? </p><p>That everything you do-- you need to do it for your audience. They are the Polaris Star.</p><p>Not in that cheesy McDonalds way that "the customer is always right".</p><p>But in the progressive way that even if the customer is wrong... How can we help them out? 'Cause it could have been us on the other side.</p><p><em>And I don’t know about you, but from where I sit Amazon looks to be doing alright. </em></p><p>So even if you didn't have a message, just your sheer obsession and dedication to serving your audience with value could make you successful at what you do. But the only problem with that is that it leaves you vulnerable in your niche to someone who is just as dedicated as you... but HAS a message.</p><p>The person, brand, company who has a message and makes it resonate with their psychographic is unstoppable. </p><p>All the insanely successful brands and artists out there are doing just that... by design... or by luck.</p><p><strong>Know your audience. Ignore the rest.</strong></p><p></p>
</div>
